past_medical_history: Gastroesophageal reflux disease, diagnosed 8 years ago
Gastroparesis, diagnosed 3 years ago, currently managed with metoclopramide
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 6 years ago
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 4 years ago
Chronic low back pain secondary to lumbar spondylosis

medications.frequency: as needed, up to four times daily

family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 74, no known neurological disease
Mother died at age 88 from pneumonia, had a mild tremor in her later years but was never formally evaluated
One brother, age 67, has essential tremor treated with propranolol
One sister, age 64, type 2 diabetes, otherwise healthy
No family history of Parkinson's disease or dementia

social_history.education: High school diploma, some community college courses
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story ranch home; two adult daughters live in the same city
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, approximately 15 pack-years, quit 20 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, 2-3 times per week
social_history.exercise: Used to walk two miles daily; over the past year has reduced to short walks around the block due to unsteadiness
social_history.hobbies: Enjoys gardening and playing cards with friends; wife reports he has been less interested in both recently

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. MMSE 28/30, losing one point on delayed recall (2/3 at 5 minutes) and one point on pentagons copy. Speech is mildly hypophonic but fluent with intact naming and repetition. No evidence of frontal release signs.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial expression mildly hypomimic bilaterally, described as masked facies. Visual fields full to confrontation. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk mildly reduced in bilateral upper extremities. Mild bilateral cogwheel rigidity at the wrists, slightly more prominent on the left than the right. Mild bilateral bradykinesia on finger tapping and rapid alternating movements, with decrement in amplitude and speed bilaterally. No resting tremor observed during examination, though patient endorses occasional hand tremor. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact in all extremities. Vibration sense mildly decreased at bilateral great toes. Proprioception intact. Pinprick intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. 2+ patellar bilaterally. 1+ Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly slow bilaterally but accurate. Rapid alternating movements mildly slowed bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait mildly slow with reduced arm swing bilaterally. Stride length mildly shortened. Mild difficulty with tandem gait. Slight unsteadiness on turns but no festination. Pull test shows two steps of retropulsion, recovers independently. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No myoclonus. No fasciculations. Glabellar tap sign positive (Myerson sign). No vertical gaze palsy. No cerebellar ataxia. Handwriting sample shows mild micrographia.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive gait unsteadiness and bilateral stiffness over the past year
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Darnell Henderson is a 75-year-old right-handed retired postal worker who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by his wife, Helen, for evaluation of progressive gait difficulty and generalized stiffness that has been worsening over the past twelve to fourteen months. His wife first noticed changes approximately a year ago when he began walking more slowly and appeared less steady on his feet, particularly when navigating around furniture or stepping off curbs. Over the ensuing months, he has developed bilateral stiffness that he describes as feeling like his arms and legs are heavy and resistant to movement, worse in the mornings and improving somewhat with activity. He has not noticed a clear resting tremor, though his wife reports that she has occasionally seen his left hand trembling slightly when he is sitting and watching television, which he attributes to fatigue. He has noticed that his handwriting has become smaller and more cramped over the past six months, and he has had increasing difficulty buttoning his shirt in the mornings. His voice has become quieter according to family members, though he himself is not aware of this change. He has had two near-falls in the past three months, both occurring when he attempted to turn quickly while walking, but he has not actually fallen. His wife reports that he has become somewhat slower in his movements overall, taking longer to get dressed, to eat meals, and to complete household tasks. She has also noted that his facial expression seems less animated than it used to be, and friends have commented that he looks tired or unwell even when he feels fine. He continues to drive short distances in their neighborhood but has voluntarily stopped driving on the highway due to feeling less confident. He denies any urinary incontinence, significant cognitive decline, visual hallucinations, or acting out of dreams. His appetite has been reduced, which his gastroenterologist attributes to his gastroparesis, and he has lost approximately eight pounds over the past year. Of particular relevance, he has been taking metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily for the past three years for gastroparesis, which raises the important question of whether his parkinsonian symptoms may be drug-induced or represent true idiopathic Parkinson's disease. He reports that his symptoms have progressed gradually over the past year despite being on a stable dose of metoclopramide, and his wife believes the gait changes preceded the medication initiation, though she is not entirely certain of the timeline.

volumetrics.midbrain: Normal AP diameter, no hummingbird sign
volumetrics.pons_cerebellum: Normal, no pontine or cerebellar atrophy

additional_observations: No basal ganglia infarcts or lacunes
No evidence of mass lesion or abnormal enhancement
No abnormal signal in the putamina bilaterally
Subtle ventricular enlargement slightly out of proportion to sulcal widening
mri.impression: Subtle ventricular enlargement that is mildly out of proportion to the degree of cortical atrophy; while likely age-related, clinical correlation for normal pressure hydrocephalus is suggested if gait disturbance and cognitive concerns are present. No structural lesion to explain parkinsonian symptoms. No features of multiple system atrophy or progressive supranuclear palsy by imaging criteria. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL (L), Chloride 112 mEq/L (H), Glucose 118 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 7.4 % (H).

findings.location: Bilateral putamina, more pronounced on the right

signal_characteristics.uptake_pattern: Asymmetrically reduced, right greater than left
signal_characteristics.putamina: Reduced uptake bilaterally, more marked posteriorly on the right

output.impression: Abnormal DaTscan demonstrating reduced dopamine transporter uptake bilaterally in the putamina with right-sided predominance and relative preservation of caudate uptake. Clinical correlation recommended.

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.test: Post-Levodopa UPDRS-III Motor Score (1 hour after 250mg levodopa/carbidopa)

Autonomic_Reflex_Testing.test: Head-Up Tilt Table Test - Systolic BP drop at 3 minutes

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) 25 /30 (L), Category Fluency (Animals) 13 words/60 seconds (L).

Polysomnography.value: 3 episodes of complex motor behavior during REM sleep observed

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Total Sleep Time 342 minutes minutes (H), Sleep Efficiency 78% % (H), REM Without Atonia Index 32% % (H), AHI (Apnea-Hypopnea Index) 6.4 events/hour (H), REM Behavior Disorder Episodes 3 episodes of complex motor behavior during REM sleep observed  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: REM Behavior Disorder Episodes: 3 episodes of complex motor behavior during REM sleep observed (H)

output.query: metoclopramide drug-induced parkinsonism vs idiopathic Parkinson's disease differentiation

results.title: Drug-Induced Parkinsonism: A Review
results.key_finding: Drug-induced parkinsonism is the second most common cause of parkinsonism. Metoclopramide is among the most frequent causative agents. Key distinguishing features: DIP tends to be symmetric, develops within weeks to months of drug exposure, and resolves within weeks to months of drug discontinuation.

results.title: DaTscan in differentiating drug-induced parkinsonism from Parkinson's disease
results.key_finding: DaTscan (I-123 FP-CIT SPECT) shows normal dopamine transporter binding in drug-induced parkinsonism and reduced binding in Parkinson's disease, making it the most reliable imaging test to distinguish between the two conditions.

results.title: Metoclopramide-induced parkinsonism: clinical features and outcomes
results.key_finding: Chronic metoclopramide use can cause parkinsonism that may persist even after drug discontinuation, particularly in elderly patients. Some patients with metoclopramide-induced parkinsonism may have underlying subclinical PD that is unmasked by the dopamine-blocking drug.

output.summary: Metoclopramide is a well-established cause of drug-induced parkinsonism through central dopamine D2 receptor blockade. Differentiating DIP from idiopathic PD is critical and can be challenging, especially in elderly patients on chronic metoclopramide. Key differentiating features include: (1) DIP tends to be symmetric while PD is typically asymmetric; (2) DaTscan is normal in DIP but abnormal in PD; (3) DIP may resolve after drug discontinuation while PD is progressive; (4) metoclopramide may unmask underlying subclinical PD. A trial of metoclopramide discontinuation with clinical reassessment after 3-6 months is recommended, alongside DaTscan for definitive differentiation.

interactions: MAJOR: Metoclopramide is a dopamine receptor antagonist and directly opposes the mechanism of levodopa. Concurrent use significantly reduces levodopa efficacy and should be avoided.
MINOR: Metformin may slightly reduce levodopa absorption; administer at different times.
contraindications: Concurrent use with metoclopramide is a relative contraindication due to pharmacological antagonism at dopamine receptors.
warnings: Monitor for orthostatic hypotension when combining levodopa with lisinopril and tamsulosin
May cause nausea; take with food initially
alternatives: If metoclopramide cannot be discontinued, consider domperidone (not available in US) as a prokinetic alternative that does not cross the blood-brain barrier
Consider trimethobenzamide or ondansetron for gastroparesis symptom management as alternatives to metoclopramide

differential.key_features: Patient has been on metoclopramide 10 mg TID for 3 years; DIP tends to be symmetric and bilateral, which partially matches this presentation; however, gradual progression and abnormal DaTscan argue against pure DIP
Gait disturbance and mild ventricular enlargement on MRI raise this possibility; however, absence of urinary incontinence, magnetic gait pattern, and full NPH triad makes this less likely
Brother has essential tremor; however, patient's predominant symptoms are bradykinesia and rigidity rather than action tremor, and the presence of hypomimia and micrographia favors PD
Bilateral parkinsonism could suggest MSA; however, absence of early severe autonomic dysfunction, cerebellar signs, and normal autonomic testing argue against this diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including timeline of symptom onset relative to metoclopramide initiation and characterize symmetry versus asymmetry of symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Establish whether parkinsonian symptoms preceded or followed metoclopramide use

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with focus on parkinsonian features including rigidity, bradykinesia, tremor, postural stability, and gait
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral parkinsonism with mild asymmetry, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, hypomimia, and gait instability

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No structural cause for parkinsonism; incidental mild ventricular enlargement and periventricular white matter changes

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, B12, RPR, and ceruloplasmin to exclude reversible causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal results except for known diabetes-related glucose elevation; no metabolic cause for parkinsonism identified

optimal_actions.action: Order DaTscan to differentiate idiopathic PD from drug-induced parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Abnormal DaTscan with reduced bilateral putaminal uptake, confirming neurodegenerative parkinsonism and arguing against pure drug-induced parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Discontinue metoclopramide and arrange alternative prokinetic agent for gastroparesis management
Consider levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Moderate positive response to levodopa supporting PD diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Initiate carbidopa-levodopa therapy after metoclopramide discontinuation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirm no contraindications; address major interaction with metoclopramide

optimal_actions.action: Refer for polysomnography to evaluate for REM sleep behavior disorder
Arrange neuropsychological testing to establish cognitive baseline
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline MoCA with mild executive and fluency deficits consistent with PD-MCI

critical_actions: Identify metoclopramide as a potential confounding cause of parkinsonism and address its role in the differential diagnosis
Order DaTscan to differentiate idiopathic Parkinson's disease from drug-induced parkinsonism
Discontinue metoclopramide before initiating dopaminergic therapy
Order brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism including NPH
Initiate appropriate dopaminergic therapy after excluding drug-induced etiology
contraindicated_actions: Starting levodopa while continuing metoclopramide, as the dopamine receptor antagonism will oppose therapeutic effect
Prescribing additional dopamine-blocking antiemetics (prochlorperazine, promethazine) for any nausea associated with levodopa initiation
Diagnosing drug-induced parkinsonism without DaTscan confirmation and risking missed PD diagnosis
Prescribing typical antipsychotics if behavioral symptoms develop
Ordering lumbar puncture for NPH evaluation without first establishing the clinical triad
key_reasoning_points: The bilateral and relatively symmetric nature of this patient's parkinsonism is atypical for classic PD and raises the differential of drug-induced parkinsonism given chronic metoclopramide use
Metoclopramide crosses the blood-brain barrier and blocks central dopamine D2 receptors, making it a well-known cause of drug-induced parkinsonism
DaTscan is the key investigation to differentiate: normal DaTscan indicates drug-induced parkinsonism, while abnormal DaTscan with reduced striatal uptake confirms neurodegenerative parkinsonism
The moderate positive response to levodopa challenge and abnormal DaTscan together strongly support idiopathic PD rather than pure drug-induced parkinsonism
Metoclopramide may have both contributed to and masked the true severity of underlying PD symptoms
The presence of REM sleep behavior disorder on polysomnography is a strong biomarker for synucleinopathy and further supports PD over drug-induced parkinsonism
MRI findings of mild ventricular enlargement raise NPH as a differential but the absence of the full clinical triad makes this less likely
The family history of essential tremor in the brother is a potential red herring that should not distract from the parkinsonian features in this patient

metadata.primary_teaching_point: Differentiating idiopathic Parkinson's disease from drug-induced parkinsonism in a patient on chronic metoclopramide, with DaTscan as the key discriminating investigation
